coredata.dc:title: Tailoring plastic bag derived carbon dots by sulfur-nitrogen passivation for highly selective Fe (III) ion sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lestari, Ratih
Kamiya, Yuichi
Wahyuningsih, Tutik Dwi
Kartini, Indriana
coredata.dc:description: Plastic bags were used to produce carbon dots (CDs), which can contribute to solving environmental problems. The enhanced quantum yield (QY) was achieved in this study through the passivation of CDs with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N). Pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods were used to produce S-N co-doped CDs (S-N-CDs) from plastic bags. Notably, S-N-CDs significantly enhanced the QY to 16.20 %. The resulting S-N-CDs retained remarkable fluorescence performance over high ionic strengths and UV light irradiation. The incorporation of functional groups containing oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen in S-N-CDs created recognition sites that enable specific and highly selective sensing of Fe3+ ions, with a quenching ability of up to 62 %. Moreover, these S-N-CDs displayed a wide linearity in the range of 110.64–600 µM for Fe3+ ion detection. The sensing system exhibited exceptional performance in detecting Fe3+ in actual water samples with %Recovery of 90–100 % and %RSD below 0.39 %, The findings highlight the prospect of S-N-CDs from plastic bags as promising sensors for future environmental analysis, addressing the urgent need for effective and sustainable solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3+ ion detection
Plastic bag
Quantum yield
Sensor
Sulfur-nitrogen-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of CDs and S-N-CDs. Inset: the appearance of S-N-CDs in ultraviolet (right) and visible light (left).
Fluorescence spectra of a) CDs and S-N-CDs taken at λex = 320 nm and b) S-N-CDs measured at different λex (nm).
IR spectra of CDs and S-N-CDs.
Zeta potential graph of CDs and S-N-CDs.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) XRD pattern of CDs and S-N-CDs.
(a, b) TEM images taken at various scales, (c) size distribution, (d) SAED, and (e) EDS of S-N-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of S-N-CDs a) after UV light irradiation, b) after stored under ambient conditions, c) in aqueous NaCl solution, and d) in pH-varying conditions.
(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of S-N-CDs with and without metal ions, c) fluorescence intensity of S-N-CDs-Fe3+ added to a variation of metal ions.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of S-N-CDs in Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations and (b) relationship between the Fe3+ concentration and relative fluorescence intensity.
A) UV–Vis spectra, b) zeta potential distribution graph, c) IR spectra, and d) Stern–Volmer plot of S-N-CDs that interacted with Fe3+ (S-N-CDs-Fe3+).
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms occurring on S-N-CDs.
The QY comparison of CDs passivated sulfur and nitrogen in this study with those synthesized from other raw materials.
Comparison of the Fe3+ sensing capabilities of S-N-CDs in this study with previous papers.
S-N-CDs performance for detection of Fe3+ in tap and lake water.

head.ce:title: Tailoring plastic bag derived carbon dots by sulfur-nitrogen passivation for highly selective Fe (III) ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Universitas Gadjah Mada, Yogyakarta 55281, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Universitas Gadjah Mada, Yogyakarta 55281, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology Systems, National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN), South Tangerang, Banten 15314, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology Systems, National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN), South Tangerang, Banten 15314, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Earth Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo 060-0810, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Environmental Earth Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo 060-0810, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plastic bags were used to produce carbon dots (CDs), which can contribute to solving environmental problems. The enhanced quantum yield (QY) was achieved in this study through the passivation of CDs with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N). Pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods were used to produce S-N co-doped CDs (S-N-CDs) from plastic bags. Notably, S-N-CDs significantly enhanced the QY to 16.20 %. The resulting S-N-CDs retained remarkable fluorescence performance over high ionic strengths and UV light irradiation. The incorporation of functional groups containing oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen in S-N-CDs created recognition sites that enable specific and highly selective sensing of Fe3+ ions, with a quenching ability of up to 62 %. Moreover, these S-N-CDs displayed a wide linearity in the range of 110.64–600 µM for Fe3+ ion detection. The sensing system exhibited exceptional performance in detecting Fe3+ in actual water samples with %Recovery of 90–100 % and %RSD below 0.39 %, The findings highlight the prospect of S-N-CDs from plastic bags as promising sensors for future environmental analysis, addressing the urgent need for effective and sustainable solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic is a versatile material with a large molecular weight, composed primarily of carbon and hydrogen. Unfortunately, the increase in plastic use has caused plastic waste accumulation [1–5]. Plastic is resistant to natural degradation, which has significant implications for the environment. Researchers worldwide have recognized this issue and are actively investigating the use of plastic waste to produce valuable products like fuel [6], adsorbent [7], base for road construction [8], cementitious composites [9], and biochar [10]. Among commonly used plastic products, plastic bags are one of the most attractive raw materials for valuable products because of their high carbon content, ranging from 79 % to 88 % [11]. This carbon-rich nature makes plastic bags a promising raw material for synthesizing carbon nanomaterials like carbon dots (CDs).

CDs were discovered during the single-walled carbon nanotube purification process [12]. CDs possess unique properties, including high fluorescence, tunable bandgap, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, photobleaching resistance, and ease of modification with functional groups [13–15]. These characteristics have led to CDs being applied in a wide range of fields, such as sensors [16], photocatalysts [17], drug delivery systems [18], bioimaging [19], and solar cells [20].

Several studies have successfully achieved the synthesis of CDs from various plastic products [11,21–25]. For instance, Hu et al. [11] applied hydrothermal processes to produce CDs from plastic bags, which exhibited distinct blue fluorescence and a quantum yield (QY) of 4.08 %. Similarly, plastic bottles were transformed into fluorescent CDs through the pyrolysis-hydrothermal process, achieving a QY of 5.2 % [24]. Lestari et al. [17] conducted the synthesis of CDs from high-density polyethylene using pyrolysis, sonication, and hydrothermal processes, resulting in CDs with a QY of 16.16 %. A study by Kumari et al. [22] focused on synthesizing CDs from polyolefin waste using hydrothermal and sonication processes, resulting in CDs with a QY of 4.84 %. These studies demonstrate the possibility of converting plastic into CDs.

Efforts to increase the QY and reactivity of CDs have been made by passivating CDs surfaces with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N). Cruz et al. [21] conducted passivation of CDs derived from polyurethane with S, resulting in an incredible QY of 33 %. Similarly, Huang et al. [26] synthesized CDs from expanded polystyrene and passivated them with S by pyrolysis. The resulting CDs (S-CDs) exhibited a QY of 13.2 % and striking blue fluorescence. Furthermore, Das et al. [27] effectively passivated CDs with sulfur-nitrogen (S-N-CDs) using urea and κ-carrageenan, yielding a remarkably high QY of 69.27 %. Qi et al. [28] achieved a high QY of up to 68.94 % with S-N-CDs synthesized from citric acid and taurine as raw materials. Anjana et al. [29] also contributed to this advancement by synthesizing S-N-CDs with an exceptionally high QY of 78 % using citric acid and L-cysteine. These studies highlight the successful strategies employed to passivate CDs surfaces with heteroatom groups like S and N, resulting in significant improvement in QY. These improvements can expand the use of CDs in several fields, including bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics.

Heavy metal ions are notorious pollutants that pose significant environmental and health risks. The presence of metal ions in the water environment is a particular concern. One such metal ion is Fe3+, which is commonly found in the natural environment and the human body [30,31]. Excessive intake of Fe3+ ions can lead to various issues, including liver damage, heart failure, and sometimes fatality. Therefore, the determination of the concentration of Fe3+ in water has become a crucial area of research [32,33]. Some analytical methods for Fe3+ ions in water have been established, including colorimetry, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES). However, these techniques still have drawbacks, including the necessity of additional reagents, lengthy procedures, complex and expensive equipment, and the requirement of professional operators.

Recently, fluorescence (FL) quenching has gained attention as a potential method for detecting Fe3+ ions, and CDs have emerged as an intriguing candidate for FL-based sensors for this purpose [34–36]. CDs exhibit high selectivity towards Fe3+ ions because amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on CDs surfaces can form complexes with Fe3+ ions, promoting electron transfer between the d orbitals of Fe3+ and CDs [35]. Therefore, in this study, we investigated the passivation of CDs derived from plastic bags with S and N and their application as a sensor for Fe3+ ion detection.

All reagents were analytical grade and utilized as received. Hydrogen peroxide (30 wt% H2O2), thiourea, KCl, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, CoCl2, CdSO4, HgCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, FeSO4, CrCl3, FeCl3, NaCl, quinine sulfate, and sulfuric acid were obtained from Merck (Germany). The raw material for CDs was provided by Santoso Company in the form of plastic bags, which were composed mainly of high-density polyethylene (HDPE), as reported in a previous study [25]. Tap water was obtained from the inorganic chemistry laboratory at Universitas Gadjah Mada and lake water from Lembah Lake at Universitas Gadjah Mada.

A comprehensive set of imaging and spectroscopic measurements was conducted to characterize the synthesized CDs samples. The high-voltage TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit Twin) operating at 200 kV was used to capture TEM images, selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy spectra (EDS) from S-N-CDs. For the measurement of zeta potential, a Zetasizer Nano Series instrument (Malvern) was utilized. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted using a D2 Phaser XRD from Bruker. An infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Prestige 21) was utilized to acquire IR spectra. Using a spectrophotometer manufactured by Thermo Scientific, UV–Vis spectra in the 200–800 nm range were acquired. The Shimadzu RF-6000 LabSolution was used to obtain the fluorescence spectra. A Raman microscope (Renishaw) was applied to collect Raman spectra at the laser wavelength of 785 nm.

A combination of pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods was utilized to create S-N-CDs. The optimal pyrolysis and hydrothermal conditions were applied, as previously reported in our earlier studies [23,25]. Small pieces of the plastic bag were pyrolyzed at 300 °C for 4 h. The resulting pyrolysis product of approximately 0.250 g was then mixed with 0.125 g of thiourea and 15 mL of 5 wt% H2O2. The solution was placed in a high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The product was taken out from the vessel, and any residue was removed by centrifuging at 4,000 rpm for 30 min, then S-N-CDs were obtained. As a reference, unmodified CDs were also synthesized similarly to S-N-CDs without the addition of thiourea.

The quantum yield was calculated assuming that the QY of quinine sulfate as a standard was 54 %. The determination of QY was performed using Equation (1), where “A” denotes the absorbance, “F” symbolizes the fluorescent intensity, and “η” signifies the solution’s refractive index. “S” and “CDs” subscripts indicate the standard and sample, respectively.
(1) QYCDs = QYS · FCDs/FS · AS/ACDs · η2 CDs/η2 S

The stability of S-N-CDs fluorescence was investigated by varying several parameters. An aqueous solution of NaCl with different concentrations was employed for S-N-CDs stability evaluation over ion strength (0, 0.5, 1, and 2 M). S-N-CDs were added to each aqueous NaCl solution (1:30). Furthermore, S-N-CDs were exposed to UV light for several lengths of time (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 min) to examine their stability against UV light. The storage stability of S-N-CDs was also investigated based on the change in fluorescence intensity over a storage duration of up to 54 days. This was accomplished by leaving the S-N-CDs and deionized water (1:30) under ambient conditions and measuring the fluorescence at 0, 14, 28, and 54 days. The determination of stability against pH was carried out by adding hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide to the S-N-CDs solution, resulting in a range of pH values: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13. Every solution was then examined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer set to 320 nm as the excitation wavelength (λex).

The S-N-CDs sensitivity to Fe3+ ions was evaluated using an aqueous FeCl3 solution with various concentrations (0, 1, 5, 20, 40, 100, 200, 400, and 600 µM). To 6 mL of each solution, 0.2 mL of S-N-CDs was added, and then the fluorescence was measured at λex = 320 nm. To evaluate S-N-CDs selectivity for metal cations other than Fe3+, fluorescence spectra of S-N-CDs were also recorded in liquid containing each of KCl, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, CuCl2, HgCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, FeSO4, CrCl3, and CdSO4 (400 µM). In addition, a mixed solution of FeCl3 and other metal salts was utilized to investigate the presence of interference effects toward S-N-CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence intensity. The preparation was conducted by mixing 0.2 mL of S-N-CDs, 3 mL of FeCl3 solution, and 3 mL of a solution containing each of KCl, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, CuCl2, HgCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, FeSO4, CrCl3, and CdSO4 (100 µM). Fluorescence spectra were measured similarly to those in the selectivity test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing for Fe3+ ion in actual water
ce:section.ce:para: S-N-CDs performance as Fe3+ sensor was evaluated in actual water samples (tap water and lake water). About 5.8 mL of a solution comprising tap water and an aqueous FeCl3 solution with varying Fe3+ concentrations (0, 20, 60, and 100 µM) was blended into 0.2 mL of S-N-CDs. Then, fluorescence spectra were measured at λex = 320 nm. A similar procedure was also applied to detect Fe3+ ions in lake water.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were measured to analyze the S-N-CDs' optical characteristics. The appearance of S-N-CDs under UV and visible light is displayed in Fig. 1 . Under UV light, the solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence, while it appeared light yellow and transparent when exposed to visible light. S-N-CDs exhibited absorption wavelengths ranging from 200 to 400 nm, which were broader than those of unmodified CDs of 200–360 nm. This absorption was attributed to the π─π* electron transition associated with the creation of the CDs' core structure, specifically the existence of CC sp2 bonds. Additionally, the light absorption extended to 400 nm, which was credited to the electron transition of n − π* owing to the surface functional groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups. Those functional groups were verified by IR spectra, which will be discussed later. The S-N-CDs had a wider absorption than unmodified CDs, probably owing to the sulfur and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface.

Fig. 2 a clearly showed that the introduction of sulfur and nitrogen into CDs significantly increased the fluorescence intensity. Previous studies have shown that passivation of the CDs' surface with heteroatom groups can introduce additional energy levels, intensifying the CDs' fluorescence [37–40]. Furthermore, it is noted that the wavelength giving the maximum fluorescence intensity was shorter for S-N-CDs (405 nm) than for CDs (443 nm), indicating that the passivation of the CDs’ surface with both sulfur and nitrogen can enrich the functional groups owing to heteroatoms and introduce new energy levels. Consequently, S-N-CDs can absorb light with lower energy and emit light with higher energy compared to CDs (up-conversion fluorescence). This is caused by photon absorption, which generates electron-hole pairs (excitons) within CDs. These excitons can transfer their energy to other excitons, leading to the creation of a higher-energy exciton. This energy transfer process can occur with the support of abundant surface functional groups. As a result of the energy transfer, some excitons can combine to produce higher-energy photons, corresponding to shorter emission wavelengths [41].

From the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 2a, the quantum yield (QY) for S-N-CDs was determined to be 16.20 %. The significant increase in QY along with the passivation with sulfur and nitrogen resulted from the addition of heteroatoms on the CDs surface [43,44]. A high QY is essential, particularly for fluorescence-based sensor applications, as it leads to robust fluorescence intensity and enhanced sensitivity in detecting analytes. Table 1 demonstrates that S-N-CDs prepared in this study had QY comparable to those reported in previous papers. However, the QY of S-N-CDs obtained in this study was lower than that of S-N-CDs produced by Qi et al. [28] and Romero et al. [43]. This is probably because the components contained in raw materials used to generate S-N-CDs affect their QY. Plastic bags possess resistant properties and contain carbon polymers that are difficult to break down, resulting in a smaller QY compared to those prepared using citric acid [22] and broccoli precursors [36].

The S-N-CDs fluorescence spectra at various excitation wavelengths between 300 and 420 nm are shown in Fig. 2b. As depicted in Fig. 2b, a variation in the excitation wavelength was utilized to predict the source of S-N-CD fluorescence. The emission wavelength observed a bathochromic shift when the excitation wavelength was raised from 300 to 420 nm, while the emission wavelength altered from 395 to 495 nm. This demonstrates how manipulating the excitation wavelength can change the emission wavelength. Most CDs exhibit this excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior [33,45]. These findings suggest that without changing the CDs, the emission wavelength was adjustable by varying the excitation wavelength. The excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence was correlated to π*−π transitions from the graphitic structure and π*−n transitions from O − H, C = O, −SO3, and − NH2 on the S-N-CDs surface, according to IR spectra in Fig. 3 , which will be explained in detail later.

IR spectra of CDs and S-N-CDs are presented in Fig. 3. The stretching vibrations from O–H (3200–3400 cm−1), C–H sp3 (2935 and 2859 cm−1), CO (1713 cm−1), and C–O (1160 cm−1) are seen in the two samples. These vibrations clearly indicate the presence of carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Passivation of CDs with both sulfur and nitrogen (S-N-CDs) gave an extensive absorption in the 2400–3500 cm−1 range, which resulted from the overlap of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations [46]. A similar finding was also reported by Das et al. [27]. A wide absorption also appeared in the 717–1296 cm−1 range, demonstrating the overlapping of C–N, C–O, and C–S vibrations. From the spectrum, it was confirmed that S-N-CDs had amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and thiol groups. This supported the results of fluorescence and UV–Vis analysis, which suggested that there were various functional groups on the surface of S-N-CDs.

Understanding the surface characteristics of S-N-CDs was made possible by the zeta potential characterization. Fig. 4 shows the zeta potential graph for CDs and S-N-CDs. Unpassivated CDs had a zeta potential of −4.78 mV, suggesting the existence of negatively charged groups on the surface, which were hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. S-N-CDs had zeta potentials of −4.80 and +16.3 mV. The modification with sulfur and nitrogen (S-N-CDs) resulted in an additional peak at +16.3 mV, suggesting that the surface of S-N-CDs had become positively charged. Nitrogen, specifically in the form of an amine group, was responsible for contributing to this positive charge, as confirmed by the IR spectrum. The S-N-CDs zeta potential in this work was not significantly different from previous reports (−6.2 mV) [47,48]. The surface charge of S-N-CDs changes as a result of the combined effect of sulfur and nitrogen passivation, providing valuable information about surface chemistry and potential applications. The zeta potential not being highly positive or negative (above ± 30 mV) was due to the presence of nitrogen and sulfur modifications introducing functional groups (hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl) that balance the surface charge, preventing excessive accumulation of positive or negative charges. Previous studies have also reported similar findings, where the zeta potential of CDs did not exceed ± 30 mV [47,48].

The Raman spectra of CDs and S-N-CDs in Fig. 5 a illustrate two distinctive bands belonging to the G and D bands at 1500 and 1300 cm−1, respectively. These bands are commonly observed in the Raman spectra of carbon-based materials, including CDs. The D band indicates the presence of defects within the carbon structure of materials [42]. These defects can arise from dissociated carbon–carbon bonds or deformation of the graphite-like structure present in the carbon material. Conversely, the G band signifies the existence of well-ordered sp2 carbon bonds within a graphite-like structure [42]. This observation suggests that S-N-CDs possess a graphite-like structure with inherent defects, likely resulting from the incorporation of carboxyl, hydroxyl, thiol, amine, and carbonyl groups, in line with the IR spectrum. The IG/ID ratio of S-N-CDs was measured at 0.96, slightly higher than unmodified CDs at 0.93, suggesting enhanced crystallinity in S-N-CDs [49].

The XRD pattern in Fig. 5b illustrates the amorphous nature of S-N-CDs compared to thiourea, which was used as the nitrogen and sulfur precursor. Thiourea exhibits distinct crystallographic planes, including (101), (110), (002), (020), (021), (120), (312), (200), (103), (201), (103), and (004) [50,51]. In contrast, S-N-CDs show a reduction in crystallinity and peak shifts caused by the formation of a core structure and surface sites of S-N-CDs. The appearance of graphite and graphite oxide peaks [25], in line with Raman analysis, further indicates the successful transformation of thiourea into the CDs structure. Compared to the diffractogram of unmodified CDs, which exhibited a broad amorphous peak, S-N-CDs displayed sharp peaks. This indicated the crystalline features of S-N-CDs, which were also observed in the TEM and SAED analyses.

The TEM image, particle size distribution, SAED image, and EDS spectrum of S-N-CDs are displayed in Fig. 6 . S-N-CDs had a sphere-like form and were well dispersed, as illustrated in Fig. 6a. The (101), (100), and (002) planes of the graphite carbon structure were clearly observed in Fig. 6b, which is notable for showing obvious lattice fringes with spacings of 0.20, 0.25, and 0.35 nm, respectively [52,53]. These observations indicated the crystalline graphite structure of S-N-CDs. Regarding particle size analysis (Fig. 6c), S-N-CDs size ranged from 2.24 to 5.54 nm with an average of 3.87 nm.

Furthermore, Fig. 6d shows the SAED pattern, confirming the presence of 0.25 nm and 0.20 nm lattice spacings belonging to a graphite-like structure. Elemental analysis from EDS in Fig. 6e revealed that S-N-CDs contain carbon (52.35 %), oxygen (28.95 %), sulfur (0.15 %), and nitrogen (18.55 %), demonstrating the successful passivation of CDs with nitrogen and sulfur. The presence of the Cu element comes from the sample grid used for the TEM measurement. By combining all the physicochemical characterization data, we deduced that the S-N-CDs basic structure was identical to graphite and had many surface functional groups, including carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, thiol, and amino, as illustrated in Fig. 7 .

The fluorescence stability of S-N-CDs was thoroughly examined by comparing the fluorescence intensity under various conditions. Fig. 8 a displays fluorescence spectra of S-N-CDs after UV irradiation for different durations: 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 min. Surprisingly, no notable changes were observed in both the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of the S-N-CDs, even after exposure to UV light for a prolonged period of 120 min. Furthermore, there was no evidence of photobleaching or coagulation following the irradiation process. According to a previous investigation [38], these results were explained by the electrostatic attraction within the negatively charged surfaces of the S-N-CDs. Collectively, these findings clearly indicated the excellent photostability exhibited by S-N-CDs. Photostability is crucial for maintaining the performance and accuracy of the respective applications by offering accurate and reproducible results in fields such as UV microscopy, UV sensors, and UV-activated fluorescence-based assays.

Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the S-N-CDs remained stable, as seen in Fig. 8b, and they stayed well-dispersed without precipitation for up to 14 days due to the lack of ionization on their surface [54]. This compelling result underscores the exceptional photostability of S-N-CDs, which remains unaffected even during prolonged storage. However, storage for up to 54 days resulted in less rise in the fluorescence intensity of S-N-CDs due to the existence of residual H2O2 in the solution, leading to further oxidation reactions. Further, the ionic strength effect on S-N-CDs fluorescence intensity was assessed in NaCl solution. As displayed in Fig. 8c, fluorescence intensity for S-N-CDs was not significantly changed in the NaCl solution with concentrations up to 0.5 M. These results not only demonstrate the effective control of S-N-CDs' aggregation in the presence of NaCl ions but also highlight the capability of individual separation of S-N-CDs through salt (NaCl) interference. However, a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at extreme NaCl concentrations of 1.0 and 2.0 M. This can lead to interactions between ions in the solution and S-N-CDs functional groups, causing fluorescence quenching and a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Fig. 8d demonstrated that S-N-CDs had fluorescence stability at pH ranges from 3 to 11. The decrease in fluorescence intensity that occurred at extreme pH (1 and 13) was primarily due to changes in the surface structure. S-N-CDs undergo significant protonation at pH 1 and deprotonation at pH 13, which can alter the surface functional groups and lead to aggregation or changes in the electronic structure [55]. In general, according to the fluorescence stability characterization above, S-N-CDs had stable and excellent optical properties.

In this study, S-N-CDs were investigated as fluorescence sensors for metal ion detection based on the complex formation between metal ions and surface groups of S-N-CDs. The fluorescence intensity fluctuations in the existence of various metal ions were used to evaluate S-N-CDs selectivity as metal ion sensors for Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ag+, and Cd2+. The fluorescence intensity changes were observed at λex 320 nm upon the addition of various metal ion solutions with a concentration of 400 µM (Fig. 9 a). To evaluate the selectivity quantitatively, the values of F and F0, which are S-N-CDs fluorescence intensity with and without metal ions, respectively, were measured, and F/F0 was used as an indicator of the selectivity (Fig. 9b). Notably, the fluorescence intensity was significantly changed only for Fe3+ ions. It was observed that Fe3+ ions caused a stronger quenching effect on S-N-CDs fluorescence compared to other metal ions, with quenching of up to 54 % observed in 400 µM of Fe3+ ions. On the other hand, the existence of other metal ions resulted in only slight and insignificant changes in fluorescence intensity. Fe2+ ions, for example, caused a minor quenching of approximately 14 %, suggesting the formation of CD/Fe2+ complexes, albeit to a lesser extent. The selective binding of S-N-CDs to Fe3+ is primarily connected to the higher stability of the complex formed with Fe3+ compared to other metal ions. The sulfur and nitrogen atoms can form strong bonds and chelating structures with Fe3+, resulting in more stable complexes. This finding is consistent with previous research on S-N-CDs showing fluorescence quenching with Fe3+ [56].

To further reinforce the finding that S-N-CDs exhibited exceptional selectivity towards Fe3+, an interference test was further conducted using Fe3+ solution with other cations. The S-N-CDs-Fe3+ complex was added to various cations to evaluate the impact of metal interference on S-N-CDs-Fe3+ quenching, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded as shown in Fig. 9c. Remarkably, the interference test showed that other metal ions had a negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of S-N-CDs already bound to Fe3+, as demonstrated in Fig. 9c. These findings provided strong evidence that S-N-CDs serve as efficient and selective fluorescence sensors for detecting Fe3+ ions, exhibiting a higher affinity for Fe3+ compared to other metals.

The maximum allowable concentration of iron in wastewater is 5–10 mg/L (90–180 µM) [57], which remains above the LOD of S-N-CDs, thus the synthesized S-N-CDs shall be applicable for direct wastewater analysis. While for evaluating drinking water, the standard addition and spiking method was required, since the upper limit of Fe3+ in drinking water is 0.1 mg/L (1.8 µM) [58], falling below the LOD of the resulted S-N-CDs. Therefore, in the next discussion, we present the analysis of Fe(III) in tap water and lake water as the real sample analysis.

The Fe3+ ions in actual water samples, specifically tap water and lake water, were successfully detected using S-N-CDs. The samples were prepared by filtering the tap water and lake water using a 0.22-µm filter before being spiked with different amounts of Fe3+ metal ions. %Recovery refers to a calculation that assesses how accurately an analytical method can determine the amount of a specific component in a sample compared to the true or expected amount. A higher percentage recovery implies a more accurate method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) showed that the measurements were done in triplicate, and the average analysis findings were calculated. Table 3 presents that the recovery of Fe3+ from tap water samples ranged from 95 to 100 %, while that of the lake water was 90–100 %. Additionally, the RSD was found to be below 0.39 %, demonstrating the precision and reliability of the S-N-CDs sensor to detect Fe3+. The successful use of the S-N-CDs sensor on actual tap and lake water samples indicates further evidence of its usefulness in practice and demonstrates its potential as a powerful approach for detecting Fe3+ [61]. The analysis of Fe3+ ions in wastewater will be considered as the future research for the synthesized S-N-CDs.

Extensive investigations were conducted to elucidate the mechanisms for the detection of Fe3+ over S-N-CDs, utilizing zeta potential analysis and UV–Vis and IR spectroscopies. The results are shown in Fig. 11 . The absorbance of Fe3+ and S-N-CDs varied when Fe3+ was mixed with S-N-CDs, as seen in the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 11a). Fe3+ exhibits maximum absorption wavelengths at 200 and 301 nm, while S-N-CDs show them at 200 and 263 nm. Mixing Fe3+ with S-N-CDs causes a change in absorption wavelengths to 211 and 295 nm, indicating the interaction between the two [18]. This interaction is ascribed to the negatively charged groups on the S-N-CDs surface, which can bind Fe3+ and create S-N-CDs-Fe3+ complexes. Further investigation was carried out using zeta potential characterization (Fig. 11b). S-N-CDs were negatively charged particles with a negative zeta potential since the S-N-CDs surface had carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [36]. Nevertheless, the zeta potential of S-N-CDs changed from −4.80 to 10.35 mV upon the addition of Fe3+, moving towards a positive value. This change indicated that the complexes were formed through the interaction between positively charged Fe3+ ions and the negatively charged surface of S-N-CDs [25].

Further analysis using IR spectroscopy (Fig. 11c) showed that the intensity of absorption bands for N–H, O–H, CO, C–O, and C–N bonds reduced with the introduction of Fe3+, suggesting that S-N-CDs bound to Fe3+ through these functional groups. Therefore, a possible quenching mechanism of S-N-CDs-Fe3+ is proposed in Fig. 12 . The functional groups on the S-N-CDs surface, including N–H, O–H, CO, C–O, and C–N, can interact with Fe3+ ions. As a result of being excited, the many electrons in S-N-CDs then move to the partially filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+. This mechanism prevents electron recombination, which lowers the S-N-CDs fluorescence intensity [33,35]. Based on the Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 11d, an upward/positive deviation was observed in the systems, indicating that both static and dynamic quenching contributed to the decrease in fluorescence intensity. This result was consistent with the findings reported by Das et al. [27], who stated that both static and dynamic quenching processes occur in systems using S-N-CDs as sensors.

In this study, a straightforward, affordable, easy, and eco-friendly process has been successfully used to produce S-N-CDs utilizing the pyrolysis-hydrothermal method. This study utilized hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant together with thiourea and plastic bags as raw materials. Under UV light, the produced S-N-CDs glow brightly blue and exhibit wavelength-dependent excitation characteristics. The passivation of CDs with sulfur and nitrogen groups can increase fluorescence intensity compared to unmodified CDs. S-N-CDs had a quantum yield (QY) of 16.20 %. The surface functional groups of S-N-CDs included carboxyl, hydroxyl (O–H), carbonyl (CO), thiol (C–S), amino group (N–H), and C–N. S-N-CDs had a spherical form, an average size of 3.87 nm, and a core structure similar to graphite. Highly fluorescence resistant to UV radiation, ionic strength, and duration time in storage were exhibited by S-N-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of S-N-CDs was decreased by Fe3+ addition. Owing to the complex formation between S-N-CDs surface groups and Fe3+ ions, S-N-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ ions. S-N-CDs demonstrated a wide linear detection range for Fe3+ analysis of 110.64–600 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 33.19 µM. Fe3+ in the tap and lake water samples was successfully detected using S-N-CDs. We have also proved that the S-N-CDs sensor was highly accurate and trustworthy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used AI technology in order to improve readability and language. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the published article.

Ratih Lestari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuichi Kamiya: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Tutik Dwi Wahyuningsih: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Indriana Kartini: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author gratefully acknowledges the financial support from the Postdoctoral Program of Universitas Gadjah Mada, Indonesia (1688/UN1/DITLIT/PT.01.00/2024).

Chu, “Facile and cost-effective preparation of carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in living cells based on the ‘on-off-on’ fluorescence principle,” Appl Surf Sci, vol. 469, pp. 911–916, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.11.095

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ratih Lestari studied Chemistry at Universitas Gadjah Mada, Indonesia, and earned her Doctor’s degree in 2024. She is currently a young researcher at the Nanotechnology System Research Center, National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN), Indonesia. Her research focuses on the synthesis of carbon dots from plastic waste for heavy metal ion sensing applications and the development of carbon dot-based nanocomposites for photocatalytic applications. She has 6 papers and 1 book to her name.

Yuichi Kamiya received his Bachelor and Master’s degrees in Engineering at Nagoya University in 1995 and 1997, respectively. He spent his early career in Tonen Chemical Corporation (1997-2002) and Japan Science and Technology Agency (2002-2003) with positions in research and production. In 2003 he received his Doctor’s degree in Engineering at Nagoya University. In 2003 he started his academic career as Associate Professor in the Faculty of Environmental Earth Science, Hokkaido University and then he was promoted to Professor in 2015. His major research interests are environmental catalysts for the purification of water polluted with chemicals and the design of heterogeneous catalysts based on the regular assembling of well-defined metal and metal oxide clusters. He has over 126 publications to her name.

Tutik Dwi Wahyuningsih earned her Ph.D. in Chemistry from the University of New South Wales in 2004. She is currently a lecturer in the Chemistry Department at Universitas Gadjah Mada and holds the title of Professor. Her research focuses on organic synthesis, heterocyclic chemistry, green synthesis, and chemosensors. She is actively engaged in the synthesis and development of organic compounds based on green chemistry principles, particularly in the search for drug candidates, chemosensors, oleochemistry, and biosurfactants. Additionally, she investigates the bioactivity of compounds as potential drugs through in silico, in vitro, and in vivo studies. She has over 83 publications to her name.

Indriana Kartini earned her Ph.D. in Chemical Engineering from the University of Queensland, Brisbane, Australia, in 2004 and currently holds the title of Professor. She is a lecturer in the Chemistry Department at Universitas Gadjah Mada and serves as the Head of the Undergraduate Chemistry Program. Her research focuses on nanomaterials for energy and coating applications. She is actively engaged in studies related to semiconductor chemistry, photocatalysis, spectroscopy, macromolecules, material synthesis, nanomaterials, material characterization, transition metal chemistry, and solid-state chemistry, as well as their applications. She has over 97 publications to her name.
